attributes.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to the labour ward in active labour with her first child. She has been in labour for 18 hours. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears distressed. Her pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 140/90 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.8°C. Abdominal examination reveals a longitudinal lie with cephalic presentation. The fetal heart rate is 150 beats per minute. Vaginal examination shows the cervix is 6 cm dilated with no progress in the last four hours despite regular, strong contractions. The midwife notes blood-stained urine in the catheter bag.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This scenario presents a case of obstructed labour. The key features supporting this diagnosis include prolonged labour (18 hours), lack of cervical progress despite regular contractions, and the presence of blood-stained urine. Obstructed labour occurs when the fetus cannot pass through the birth canal despite strong uterine contractions, often due to cephalopelvic disproportion or malpresentation.

Normal labour progression would show steady cervical dilation and fetal descent. In this case, the lack of progress over four hours despite strong contractions indicates an abnormal labour pattern.

Precipitate labour is characterized by unusually rapid labour progression, typically lasting less than three hours from the onset of regular contractions to delivery. This is not consistent with the prolonged labour described in this scenario.

Uterine rupture is a serious complication that can occur during labour, especially in women with a previous cesarean section. While it can cause prolonged labour, it typically presents with sudden onset of severe abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding, and fetal distress. These symptoms are not present in this case.

Placental abruption involves the premature separation of the placenta from the uterine wall. It typically presents with abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding, and fetal distress. The absence of these symptoms makes this diagnosis less likely.

In obstructed labour, blood-stained urine can result from trauma to the bladder or urethra due to prolonged pressure from the fetal head. Other signs of obstructed labour, not mentioned in this scenario but important to assess, include caput formation (swelling of the fetal scalp) and excessive moulding of the fetal head. Continuous fetal heart rate monitoring is crucial in these cases to detect any signs of fetal distress. Management of obstructed labour often involves cesarean section to prevent complications such as fistula formation, uterine rupture, or fetal compromise.
learningPoints: Obstructed labour is characterized by slow or arrested cervical dilation and fetal descent despite adequate uterine contractions.
Blood-stained urine, caput formation, and excessive moulding are signs of obstructed labour.
Continuous fetal heart rate monitoring is crucial in suspected obstructed labour to detect fetal distress.
references: Gill P, Henning JM, Carlson K, Van Hook JW. Abnormal Labor. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 [updated 2023 Jun 14]. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459260/
